title: Master Theorem (Reference)
whenToUse: Only as a cross-check. The professor explicitly says this is OPTIONAL and the course uses recursion trees + induction. Many recurrences on the exam don't even fit the master-theorem form.
keyFormulas: Recurrence form: $T(n) = a T(n/b) + f(n)$ with $a \ge 1,\ b > 1$
professor:
**Theorem (Master Theorem).** Let $T(n) = a T(n/b) + f(n)$ with $a \ge 1$, $b > 1$ constants and $f$ asymptotically positive. Let $\alpha = \log_b a$.

- **Case 1 (leaf-dominated).** If $f(n) = O(n^{\alpha - \epsilon})$ for some $\epsilon > 0$, then $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\alpha})$.
- **Case 2 (balanced).** If $f(n) = \Theta(n^{\alpha})$, then $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\alpha} \log n)$.
- **Case 3 (root-dominated).** If $f(n) = \Omega(n^{\alpha + \epsilon})$ for some $\epsilon > 0$ AND the regularity condition $a f(n/b) \le c f(n)$ for some $c < 1$ holds for large $n$, then $T(n) = \Theta(f(n))$.

**Intuition.** Compare $f(n)$ with $n^{\alpha}$ (the 'leaf-work' exponent). Whichever dominates, wins.

**Examples.**

- $T(n) = 9T(n/3) + n$: $\alpha = 2$, $f(n) = n = O(n^{2 - 1})$ — Case 1. $T(n) = \Theta(n^2)$.
- $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$: $\alpha = 1$, $f(n) = n = \Theta(n^1)$ — Case 2. $T(n) = \Theta(n \log n)$ (Merge Sort).
- $T(n) = 3T(n/2) + n$: $\alpha = \log_2 3$, $f(n) = n = O(n^{\log_2 3 - \epsilon})$ — Case 1. $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_2 3})$ (Karatsuba).
- $T(n) = 3T(n/4) + n \log n$: $\alpha = \log_4 3 \approx 0.79$. $f(n) = n \log n = \Omega(n^{\alpha + \epsilon})$ for small $\epsilon$. Regularity holds. Case 3 → $\Theta(n \log n)$.

**Limitations.**

- Does not apply when the gap between $f(n)$ and $n^{\alpha}$ is not polynomial (e.g. $f(n) = n^{\alpha} / \log n$).
- Does not apply to recurrences with unequal splits like $T(n) = T(n/5) + T(7n/10) + n$. For those, use recursion trees.

**Course guidance.** The professor explicitly marks this as optional and recommends using recursion trees + induction instead. You will not be required to use the master theorem on the exam — but it's a useful **sanity check**.
simplified:
**One-line version:** for $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$, compute $\alpha = \log_b a$. Compare $f(n)$ with $n^\alpha$.

- $f \ll n^\alpha$ (polynomially smaller) → $\Theta(n^\alpha)$.
- $f \approx n^\alpha$ → $\Theta(n^\alpha \log n)$.
- $f \gg n^\alpha$ (polynomially larger) → $\Theta(f(n))$.

**The three recurrences you need:**

- Merge Sort: $2T(n/2)+n = \Theta(n\log n)$. (Case 2.)
- Karatsuba: $3T(n/2)+n = \Theta(n^{\log_2 3})$. (Case 1.)
- Binary search: $T(n/2)+1 = \Theta(\log n)$. (Case 2 with $\alpha = 0$.)

**Don't rely on this for the exam** — the course uses recursion trees + induction. Many of the exam recurrences (e.g. $T(n) = T(n/2) + T(n/3) + n$) can't even be solved via master theorem.
